title: ADK-404: Enterprise Sandbox Security & Isolation
description: Isolate agent execution nodes with gVisor, enforce strict syscall filter policies, limit egress connections, and monitor trails.

highlights.title: gVisor Isolation
highlights.description: Deploy agents using gVisor container runtimes to intercept host kernel requests from custom tools.

highlights.title: Syscall Filtering
highlights.description: Enforce strict seccomp filtering to block file system write or executing permissions from agents.

highlights.title: Egress Controls
highlights.description: Restrict outbound network ports, locking agent tool operations within internal network zones.

modules.title: 1. Sandboxing strategies: Linux namespaces and cgroups

blocks.content: Linux Namespaces and Cgroups

Running untrusted agent code requires isolation. Namespaces partition system resources (network interfaces, mounts, process lists), and cgroups limit compute resource quotas (CPU, memory), preventing malicious loops from exhausting host power.
Namespaces & Limits

blocks.code:
# Run agent container with strict memory limits
docker run --memory="512m" --cpus="1.0" --network=none adk-agent-image

blocks.content: Check Your Understanding

Question 1: What linux mechanism restricts an agent's memory usage limits?
- A) Namespaces
- B) Control Groups (cgroups)
- C) Seccomp filters

Feedback: Option B is correct. cgroups throttle and limit hardware resources.

modules.title: 2. Configuring gVisor runtime for untrusted tool code

blocks.content: gVisor Runtime Security

Containers share the host kernel. If an agent compromises a tool, it can exploit kernel vulnerabilities to escape. gVisor runs a user-space kernel (Sentry), intercepting syscalls to isolate host systems.
Docker Run with gVisor

blocks.code:
# Execute agent inside runsc (gVisor) runtime
docker run --runtime=runsc -it adk-agent-sandbox

blocks.title: Sentry Core
blocks.content: gVisor's Sentry implements a large portion of the Linux kernel API in user-space, preventing direct communication with the hardware layer.
Check Your Understanding

Question 1: How does gVisor block container escape exploits?
- A) It encrypts network traffic.
- B) It runs a user-space kernel proxy intercepting system calls before they reach the host.
- C) It requires OAuth tokens for execution.

Feedback: Option B is correct. User-space kernel proxying blocks escape exploits.

modules.title: 3. Restricting network egress and system calls

blocks.content: Egress & Syscall Restricting
To prevent agents from leaking database records, egress firewalls must restrict outbound traffic. Only allow queries to approved API endpoints.

blocks.title: Network Lockdowns
blocks.content: Enforce policies that block DNS requests to unrecognized hosts, blocking data exfiltration attempts through DNS tunnel packets.
Check Your Understanding

Question 1: Why enforce strict egress boundaries on agent execution containers?
- A) To reduce memory allocation size.
- B) To block compromised tools from exfiltrating data to external hosts.
- C) To make compilation faster.

Feedback: Option B is correct. Firewalls prevent exfiltration.

modules.title: 4. Monitoring, logging, and auditing agent tool executions

blocks.content: Audit Logging Protocols
Enterprise governance requires detailed audit records. Log every tool call, argument, returned JSON blob, and executing user session identifier.
Secure Log Entry Schema
Check Your Understanding

Question 1: What details are required in enterprise agent audit records?
- A) Program build variables only.
- B) User identity, invoked tool name, exact arguments, and permission status.
- C) The API token string.

Feedback: Option B is correct. Complete session context is required for security review.

modules.title: 5. Lab: Hardening a Docker run environment

blocks.content: Lab: Hardening a Docker Run Environment
In this lab, you will configure a shell script that launches an agent container using cgroups limits and disabled networking options.
Step 1: Write the Launch Script

blocks.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Hardened docker run script
docker run \
  --name "hardened-agent" \
  --network "none" \
  --read-only \
  --memory "256m" \
  --cpus "0.5" \
  -v $(pwd)/workspace:/workspace:ro \
  adk-agent-image:latest

blocks.content: Check Your Understanding

Question 1: What permission boundary is set on the mounted workspace folder in the script?
- A) Write only
- B) Read Only (:ro)
- C) Executable only

Feedback: Option B is correct. Read-only mounting prevents agents from modifying files.
